See discussions, stats, and author profiles for this publication at: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/266415994



Hexagonal discrete global grid systems for geospatial computing

Article in Archives of Photogrammetry Cartography and Remote Sensing · January 2011



CITATIONS                                                                                                 READS
97                                                                                                        1,537


1 author:

            Kevin Sahr
            Southern Oregon University
            20 PUBLICATIONS 1,929 CITATIONS

              SEE PROFILE




 All content following this page was uploaded by Kevin Sahr on 29 January 2022.

 The user has requested enhancement of the downloaded file.
              Archives of Photogrammetry, Cartography and Remote Sensing, Vol. 22, 2011, pp. 363-376


          HEXAGONAL DISCRETE GLOBAL GRID SYSTEMS FOR GEOSPATIAL
                               COMPUTING
                                                               K. Sahr

      Dept. of Computer Science, Southern Oregon University, Ashland, Oregon, 97520 USA – sahrk@sou.edu


KEY WORDS: Mapping, Integration, Modeling, Data Structures, Representation, Global, Hierarchical, Multiresolution

ABSTRACT:

Advanced geospatial applications often involve complex computing operations performed under sometimes severe resource
constraints. These applications primarily rely on traditional raster and vector data structures based on square lattices. But there is
a significant body of research that indicates that data structures based on hexagonal lattices may be a superior alternative for
efficient representation and processing of raster and vector data in high performance applications. The advantages of hexagonal
rasters for image processing are discussed, and hexagonal discrete global grid systems for location coding are introduced. The
combination provides an efficient, unified approach to location representation and processing in geospatial systems.


                     1. MOTIVATION                                    raster and vector data structures, as well as those factors that
                                                                      have so far inhibited more widespread adoption of hexagon-
Advanced geospatial applications, such as mobile mapping,             based representations for geospatial applications.
often perform complex spatial operations on potentially large
data sets, with strict controls on the accuracy of internal                   2. HEXAGONAL IMAGE PROCESSING
location representations, and in computing environments that
may be severely constrained by resource and size limitations.         As a basis for photogrammetry and general image
These systems therefore often place a premium on                      processing, it is not an exaggeration to state that raster grids
representational and algorithmic efficiency, and are in a             consisting of hexagonal pixels, arranged in a hexagonal
constant state of improvement as more efficient                       topology, are superior to those based on square pixels of
representations and algorithms become available. Among the            equivalent frequency under virtually every efficiency and
most fundamental data structures are those used for the               geometric metric. Hexagon rasters are 13.4% more efficient
representation and storage of raster image data and vector            at sampling circularly bandlimited signals (Petersen &
geospatial location data. Because they are so pervasive, even         Middleton, 1962), and processing algorithms on hexagon
small improvements in efficiency or representational                  rasters are 25-50% more efficient (Mersereau, 1979).
accuracy in these data structures can result in substantial           Staunton (1989) implemented a set of edge detection
performance increases in an overall system.                           operators on a hexagonal raster and realized over 40% better

Data structures for the representation and storage of raster          grids.
and vector data in geospatial applications have traditionally
been built on substrates of square lattices. The common               These efficiencies are closely tied to the unique geometric
standards have long been raster grids of square pixels and            attributes of a hexagonal lattice. Hexagons have the highest
vector coordinates consisting of 2- or 3-tuples of floating           symmetry and are the most circular of all regular polygons
point values.                                                         that tile the plane (Yale, 1968). Davies (1984) noted that
                                                                      operators defined on square rasters may be dominated by
Yet if the goal is optimal representational and algorithmic           preferred horizontal and vertical directions, leading to
efficiency and superior semantic expressiveness, then data            anisotropy in the operators’ spectral properties, and argued
structures based on squares are likely not the best choice.           instead for isotropic hexagon raster operators that exhibit
There is a substantial body of research that indicates that           “circularity” (see also Coleman et al., 2004; Scotney &
representations based on hexagonal lattices are superior, and         Coleman, 2007). Hexagon lattices have uniform and
such research has arrived at this conclusion consistently             unambiguous connectivity, with each pixel having six
across a number of research areas that directly apply to              neighbors with which it shares an edge, and whose centers
geospatial systems, such as photogrammetry, image                     are equidistant from its center. In contrast, a pixel in a square
processing, and geospatial location coding. Research in               lattice has two types of neighbors: four pixels with which it
hexagonal image processing and pattern recognition has been           shares an edge, and four pixels with which it shares a vertex,
on-going for over 40 years, and has recently seen a sharp             and the centers of the two types of neighbors are different

research in hexagon-based location coding has culminated in           semantic paradoxes when dealing with boundaries on square

(Sahr et al., 2003): multi-resolution, hierarchically indexed         n-order neighborhoods on a hexagon raster allows for greater
location systems that seamlessly tile the globe.                      angular resolution (Golay, 1969). These neighborhoods are

development on hexagon rasters. Examples of hexagon raster

and field-of-view (Verbrugge, 1997), parallel pattern            map projection space. Operations on these tuples are usually

(Snyder et al., 1999; Gardiner et al., 2009; Shima et al,        tuples of real numbers. While undoubtedly useful, this

hexagonal rasters; these include a time projection               class with respect to geospatial location. The question of the
spectrometer (Anderson, 1979) and silicon drift detector         optimal arrangement of these fixed points can be framed as a

ghosting (Hoedlmoser et al., 2009). I can only speculate on      represented by mapping them to the nearest of these fixed

will become more widely available. However, given the            arrangements of these fixed points. We can determine which
numerous significant advantages of such devices it is likely     arrangement has the smallest average quantization error. Or
that their use will continue to grow.                            we can treat each fixed point as the center of a circular region

The dearth of hexagonal sensor and display devices has led       least overlap, or the arrangement with no overlap but with the
to a proliferation of algorithms for resampling between          least uncovered area. The provably optimal solution to all of
square and hexagon rasters (see the comprehensive survey         these formulations is to arrange the fixed points as the center
and comparison in Gardiner et al., 2010), and of algorithms

points of a hexagonal lattice (Rogers, 1964; Conway &             using very efficient per-digit table lookups (Bell & Holroyd,
Sloane, 2010).                                                    1991).

Given an optimal hexagonal lattice representation of raster or
vector location, we next turn our attention to the problem of
efficiently assigning addresses to these locations.

          4. INDEXING HEXAGONAL GRIDS

In contrast to the two orthogonal axes of square-lattice based
coordinate systems, hexagon lattices have three natural axes
spaced 120° apart, as illustrated in Figure 1. Any two of these
axes are sufficient to uniquely identify each hexagon using a     !    Figure 2. Example recursive aggregation units with
2-tuple of integers.                                                            apertures 3, 4, and 7 respectively.

It is often useful to assign to each hexagon a linear code or     Aperture 7 aggregation best approximates a hexagonal shape
index. The most useful indexes are hierarchical prefix codes,     and has therefore received the most attention. The most
where the cell being indexed is considered to be at a specific    widely-used digit assignment for each aperture 7 unit is
resolution in a multi-resolution structure, and each digit in     Generalized Balanced Ternary (GBT) (Gibson & Lucas,
the index corresponds to a location at a single resolution        1982) (an alternate aperture 7 digit assignment is given in the
relative to a hierarchical parent’s index. Such an indexing       spiral addressing of Middleton & Sivaswamy, 2005). GBT is
implicitly defines both a locality-preserving total ordering of   a generalization of one-dimensional Balanced Ternary
the pixels and a pyramid data structure, and enables the          addressing (Knuth, 1998), which uses three-valued digits that
development of efficient hierarchical algorithms. The             represent -1, 0, or 1. As illustrated in Figure 3, GBT
canonical example of a hierarchical prefix code is the square     generalizes this notation to the three axes of a hexagon grid.
quadtree (Gargantini, 1982), where a square is recursively        In any seven-hex unit the central hex is designated digit 0.
sub-divided into 4 smaller squares, each of which is assigned     The digits 1 through 6 are arranged so that, if the digits are
an index consisting of the parent square’s index concatenated     stored as 3-bit binary values, digits on opposite sides of the
with one of the digits 1, 2, 3, or 4. Hierarchical prefix         central hex are binary complements of each other, allowing
location codes naturally encode both direction and precision,     negation to be performed efficiently using the binary
without the need for metadata, and provide an implicit            complement operation. Depending upon the application, the
algorithm for feature generalization through address              remaining unused possibility per 3-bit digit, base-10 digit 7,
truncation (Dutton, 1999).                                        can be used to represent the aggregate group of seven child
                                                                  cells associated with the indexed cell (Gibson & Lucas,
                                                                  1982), to efficiently indicate address termination in a variable
                                                                  length index, or to indicate that all higher resolution digits
                                                                  are zero, efficiently communicating with a finite number of
                                                                  digits that the index exactly represents the center point of the
                                                                  cell with infinite precision (Sahr, 2008). Common vector
                                                                  operations, such as addition and scaling, have been defined
                                                                  on GBT using very efficient per-digit table lookups.




             Figure 1. Natural hexagonal axes.

While a square quadtree can be formed equivalently via top-
down recursive partitioning or bottom-up aggregation of
squares into larger squares, it is impossible to exactly
partition a hexagon into smaller hexagons or to aggregate
smaller hexagons to form a larger hexagon. The pixels in a                       Figure 3. GBT digit assignment.
hexagonal raster can be aggregated into groups that tile the
plane, with the centroids of these aggregates forming the         The nodes of these aggregation hierarchies can equivalently
nodes of a new hexagonal grid to which the aggregation            be viewed top-down as a multi-resolution series of hexagonal
scheme can be applied recursively. Groups of 3, 4, or 7 are       grids, as illustrated in Figure 4. Relative to the next coarser
considered the most useful; the number of pixels in an            resolution grid, the cells at each finer resolution of an
aggregate is referred to as the aperture of the hierarchy.        aperture a grid have 1/a the area and an inter-cell spacing a
Examples are given in Figure 2. A unique hierarchical prefix      factor of 1/√a smaller.
code index can then be assigned to each pixel by beginning
with the coarsest aggregates and traversing the aggregation
tree down to the individual pixels, consistently assigning
digits at each level, with the digit base traditionally
determined by the aperture (Burt, 1980; Bell & Holroyd,
1991). An arithmetic can be defined on these indexes using
               Archives of Photogrammetry, Cartography and Remote Sensing, Vol. 22, 2011, pp. 363-376




    Figure 4. Multi-resolution hexagonal grids of aperture
                   3, 4, and 7 respectively.

The top-down approach to hexagonal hierarchies can be
traced to Christaller (1966), who argued that ideal human
settlement patterns form mixed aperture 3, 4, and 7
hierarchies; Woldenberg (1979) argues that this is also true of
naturally occurring branching structures such as rivers.
Dacey (1965) gave a mathematical formulation of these
hierarchies as a multi-resolution series of lattices. White et al.
(1992) developed a computer program that generates mixed
hexagon hierarchies on a hexagonal face of a truncated
icosahedron and then inversely projects these hexagons to the
surface of the earth, indexing the cells as per the aggregation         Figure 5. Multi-resolution quantization with sub-pixel
approaches described above. Anchoring the hierarchy to the                    accuracy on an aperture 3 CPI hierarchy.
earth’s surface in this way fixes the size of the coarsest grid
resolution (unlike the traditional raster case where the grid               5. DISCRETE GLOBAL GRID SYSTEMS
sizes are determined by the size of the finest resolution
                                                                     Multi-resolution hexagonal grids have been defined on the
pixels); thus mixed aperture hierarchies provide finer control
                                                                     surface of regular polyhedra, such as the icosahedron, and
over the choice of grid cell size and spacing.
                                                                     then projected onto the sphere to create multi-resolution
                                                                     raster and vector geospatial data structures that are global in
Aggregation-based indexing works well for hierarchically
                                                                     extent without singularities. These systems are known as
indexing a single resolution raster, but it does not provide a
                                                                     discrete global grid systems (DGGSs) (Sahr et al., 2003);
true multi-resolution encoding for vector locations (Sahr,
                                                                     figure 6 illustrates an example. Note that it is impossible to
2008). In aperture 3 and 4 grids many cells overlap more
                                                                     tile the globe with hexagons; for example, if the base
than one cell at the next coarser resolution (see Figure 4), and
                                                                     polyhedron is an icosahedron then there will be exactly
each particular aggregation scheme arbitrarily chooses one of
                                                                     twelve pentagonal cells, centered on the icosahedron vertices,
those coarser cells as the indexing parent. So while
                                                                     at all resolutions.
truncating the index of a cell will yield a valid cell index at a
coarser grid resolution, that coarser cell is not necessarily the
correct quantification of the vector location at that resolution.
This also means that we cannot perform a coarse filter
equality comparison by using the highest order digits of two
indexes. Note that this is also true of addresses in a
traditional decimal number system representation. For
example, the one-digit truncation of decimal value 1.9 is 1,
while the discrete unit quantization of that value is 2.

Note that the cells that are potentially the aperture 3 or 4
indexing children of a particular cell form a 7-hex unit, just          Figure 6. Three resolutions of an aperture 3 hexagonal
as in the aperture 7 case. We can therefore apply the GBT                         DGGS defined on an icosahedron.
indexing arrangement to create a uniform indexing scheme
for pure and mixed aperture hierarchies which I call central         The author has developed a software program called
place indexing (CPI) (Sahr, 2010). For vector locations, such        DGGRID (Sahr, 2002) for generating aperture 3 and 4
indexes encode a true multi-resolution quantization with sub-        hexagonal DGGSs with pyramid indexing that has been used
pixel accuracy (see Figure 5). They also provide a uniform           in research and data set production (e.g., Suess et al., 2004;
addressing system for aggregation schemes involving one or           Cressie & Johannesson, 2008; Hoffmann et al., 2010).
more tiling units (e.g., Sahr, 2008). CPI allows optimal             DGGSs using hierarchical aggregation indexing schemes
control of resolution while maintaining the efficient binary         have been proposed for both apertures 3 and 4 (White, 2000;
encoding and integer arithmetic approach of GBT.                     Sahr, 2008; Vince, 2009; Tong et al., 2010). CPI has also
                                                                     been extended to the sphere (Sahr, 2010) to provide a
                                                                     uniform indexing for pure and mixed aperture DGGSs, and a
                                                                     CPI grid has been designed and implemented to meet the
                                                                     narrow inter-cell spacing requirements of the U.S.
              Archives of Photogrammetry, Cartography and Remote Sensing, Vol. 22, 2011, pp. 363-376


Environmental Protection Agency Emergency Response                and groups. 3rd ed., Springer Verlag, New York, 703 pp.
Atlas project (Sahr & White, 2010).
                                                                  Cressie, N., Johannesson, G., 2008. Fixed rank kriging for
                    5. CONCLUSIONS                                very large spatial data sets. Journal of the Royal Statistical
                                                                  Society: Series B (Statistical Methodology), 70(1), pp. 209–
In this paper I attempted to demonstrate that raster and vector   226.
data structures based on hexagonal lattices offer significant
advantages over those based on square lattices. Hexagonal         Dacey, M.F., 1965. The geometry of central place theory.
lattices are now definable on multiple scales, from the entire    Geografiska Annaler: Series B, Human Geography, 47(2),
globe down to individual sensor arrays, providing an efficient    pp. 111-124.
unified approach to location representation for geospatial
computing applications. The primary obstacles to their            Davies, E.R., 1984. Circularity - a new principle underlying
adoption have been the lack of commercially available sensor      the design of accurate edge orientation operators. Image and
and display devices, and the inertia created by the long          Vision Computing, 2(3), pp. 134 -142.
history of widespread use of square-based location
representations, with the convenience and familiarity that        Delbrueck, T., 1993. Silicon retina with correlation-based,
engenders. Since no technical limitations now exist I believe     velocity-tuned pixels. IEEE Transactions on Neural
that it is only a matter of time before hexagonal raster and      Networks, 4(3), pp. 529-541.
vector representations become more widely adopted.
